name: Discovering Notre-Dame de la Goutte in Montardit
name_original: A la découverte de Notre-Dame de la Goutte à Montardit
region_intro: Touring cycling routes around Tourtouse offer a diverse landscape characterized by rolling hills and scenic views, including glimpses of the Pyrenees. The region features varied terrain suitable for different cycling preferences, from routes exploring local landmarks to those traversing more elevated areas. This area provides a mix of challenging climbs and more moderate paths. Best touring cycling routes around Tourtouse The most popular touring cycling route is Discovering Notre-Dame de la Goutte in Montardit, a 16.3 miles (26.2 km) trail that takes 1 hour 29 minutes to complete. This route explores a cultural landmark within the Montardit area. Another top favourite among local touring cyclists is View of Col du Cap Blanc loop from Barjac, a difficult 11.6 miles (18.7 km) path. This route offers views from Col du Cap Blanc, featuring varied elevation. Local touring cyclists also love the View of the Pyrenees – Great view of Saint-Girons loop from Lasserre, a 23.6 miles (37.9 km) trail leading through areas with expansive views of the Pyrenees, often completed in about 2 hours 29 minutes. Touring cycling around Tourtouse is defined by rolling hills, panoramic views of the Pyrenees, and routes connecting local villages. The network offers options for easy, moderate, and difficult ability levels. The routes in Tourtouse are highly rated by the komoot community with an average score of 4.3 stars from more than 80 reviews. More than 700 touring cyclists have used komoot to explore Tourtouse's varied terrain.
region_faq:
Q: What is the general difficulty level of touring cycling routes in Tourtouse?
A: Tourtouse offers a diverse range of touring cycling routes. You'll find options for various skill levels, with approximately 4 easy routes, 30 moderate routes, and 45 difficult routes. The region is characterized by rolling hills and varied terrain, providing both gentle rides and more challenging climbs.

Q: Are there any longer touring cycling routes available in Tourtouse?
A: Yes, for those looking for longer rides, Tourtouse has several options. One notable route is the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/26213806" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;View of the Pyrenees – Great view of Saint-Girons loop from Lasserre&lt;/a&gt;, which spans 23.6 miles (37.9 km) and offers expansive views of the Pyrenees.

Q: Are there family-friendly touring cycling routes in Tourtouse?
A: While many routes feature rolling hills, there are approximately 4 easy routes suitable for families or those preferring a less strenuous ride. These routes typically explore local landmarks and offer a more relaxed pace.

Q: What is the best time of year for touring cycling in Tourtouse?
A: The region's climate, with its rolling hills and scenic views, is generally pleasant for cycling from spring through autumn. Summer offers longer daylight hours, while spring and autumn provide milder temperatures and often vibrant landscapes. Winter cycling is possible but may involve colder conditions and potentially more challenging terrain.

Q: What kind of views can I expect on touring cycling routes in Tourtouse?
A: Many routes in Tourtouse offer panoramic views, including stunning glimpses of the Pyrenees mountains. For example, the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/36267407" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;View of Col du Cap Blanc loop from Barjac&lt;/a&gt; provides excellent vistas from Col du Cap Blanc, while the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/26213806" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;View of the Pyrenees – Great view of Saint-Girons loop from Lasserre&lt;/a&gt; is specifically known for its expansive Pyrenean views.

Q: Are there any circular touring cycling routes in Tourtouse?
A: Yes, many of the touring cycling routes in Tourtouse are designed as loops, allowing you to start and end at the same point. Examples include the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/15015764" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;View of Col du Cap Blanc – Tourtouse loop from Lasserre&lt;/a&gt; and the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/6981483" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Sainte-Croix-Volvestre loop from Sainte-Croix-Volvestre&lt;/a&gt;.

Q: What can I see along the touring cycling routes in Tourtouse?
A: Beyond the natural scenery, touring cycling routes in Tourtouse can lead you to various points of interest. You might encounter cultural landmarks like Notre-Dame de la Goutte, explored on the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e931651170/discovering-notre-dame-de-la-goutte-in-montardit" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Discovering Notre-Dame de la Goutte in Montardit&lt;/a&gt; route. The region also features historical sites and charming villages.

Q: Are there any interesting landmarks or attractions near Tourtouse that I can explore?
A: Yes, the area around Tourtouse offers several interesting attractions. You can explore the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/3437642" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;V81 Greenway Tunnels (Saint-Girons – Foix)&lt;/a&gt;, visit the historic &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/5780979" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Saint-Lizier Historic Village&lt;/a&gt;, or experience the local charm of &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/2300662" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Montbrun-Bocage Village Center and Market&lt;/a&gt;.

Q: What do other touring cyclists enjoy the most about touring cycling in Tourtouse?
A: The touring cycling experience in Tourtouse is highly rated by the komoot community, with an average score of 4.3 stars from over 80 reviews. Cyclists often praise the diverse landscape, the panoramic views of the Pyrenees, and the well-connected routes that link local villages, offering options for all ability levels.

Q: How many touring cycling routes are available in the Tourtouse area?
A: There are approximately 79 touring cycling routes available for exploration in the Tourtouse area, offering a wide variety of distances and difficulty levels to suit different preferences.

Q: Are there any specific routes that highlight cultural landmarks?
A: Yes, the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e931651170/discovering-notre-dame-de-la-goutte-in-montardit" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Discovering Notre-Dame de la Goutte in Montardit&lt;/a&gt; route is specifically designed to explore a significant cultural landmark within the Montardit area, offering a blend of cycling and local heritage.

Q: What kind of terrain can I expect on the touring cycling routes?
A: The terrain around Tourtouse is characterized by rolling hills, which means you can expect a mix of ascents and descents. The routes traverse varied landscapes, from open areas offering panoramic views to paths connecting charming local villages.
tips: Tourtouse is a rural commune with a population of 158 in 2020, with a population peak of 1,545 in 1831. It is part of the Saint-Girons attraction area. The inhabitants are called the Tourtousains or Tourtousains.

As the name suggests, the place was damp and water dripped through it.
This pretty chapel was built between 1968 and 2001 by Father Piquemal.
He first restored a Romanesque chapel near Arnave, near Tarascon (not that of Tartarin, the Ariège Tarascon) where a shelter was built for a miraculous stone reputed to cure epilepsy.
He was later appointed to Montardit and took care of the restoration of the church of Sainte-Croix and then restored this chapel.
 “I started picking up pebbles, then stones all over the place, then I looked for a mason, he was masonry, I was doing the work... I was picking up pebbles, I was cutting beautiful stones... with the relentlessness of gold diggers. When the chapel was finished, I still had reserves of strength to build a Stations of the Cross outside. I had stones in mind. While walking through the woods in the area, I found large stones and wanted to take them away and put them on display. The idea that they could remain anonymous in the depths of the forests, under the foliage and the earth, seemed unfair to me. »
In 1985 the chapel became an official place of pilgrimage.

This very original site exudes a lovely atmosphere, even for non-Catholics, and is worth a detour.
